lines.text: SEGMENT 5 — Ipilimumab: the first brake-cutter
Let's spend a little time on ipilimumab — Yervoy — because it was the first, and the first is always interesting.
Also because ipilimumab teaches you, in concentrated form, both the magic and the cost of checkpoint inhibition.
Ipilimumab is a fully human monoclonal antibody.
Its target is CTLA-4 on T cells.
Its mechanism: it binds to CTLA-4 and physically blocks CTLA-4 from binding B7 on antigen-presenting cells.
Without that binding, CTLA-4 can't deliver its inhibitory signal.
Cipla — sorry, CD28 — wins the competition for B7.
The T cell stays activated.
The brake is cut.
There may also be a second mechanism.
Some recent work suggests that ipilimumab, by virtue of its particular antibody isotype, can actively recruit immune cells to kill regulatory T cells inside tumors.
Regulatory T cells express CTLA-4 constitutively, and the antibody, bound to them, makes them targets for antibody-dependent cellular cytotoxicity.
So ipilimumab may be both releasing the brake on conventional T cells and depleting the suppressive T-reg population in the tumor.
The full mechanism is still being worked out.
What's clear is what it does in patients.
The pivotal trial for ipilimumab, called MDX010-20, was published in the New England Journal in 2010.
It enrolled six hundred and seventy-six patients with advanced metastatic melanoma — patients who had already failed standard treatments, patients with a median survival measured in months.
They got ipilimumab, with or without a vaccine, or the vaccine alone.
The headline result: median overall survival went from six and a half months in the control arm to ten months in the ipilimumab arm.
Three and a half months of extra median survival.
If you stop there, you might say: that's a modest result.
A few months.
Not earth-shattering.
And in fact, when the trial first reported, some oncologists were unimpressed.
But the median is the wrong number to look at.
Here's the right number.
In the ipilimumab arm, about twenty percent of patients were still alive at three years.
About eighteen percent were still alive at four years.
And then the curve went flat.
The patients who responded — about one in five — appeared to be cured.
Not improved, not extended, but cured.
People with metastatic melanoma, who had every right to be dead in a year, were attending their children's weddings ten years later.
This is the pattern that ipilimumab introduced, and that all subsequent checkpoint inhibitors have, in various ways, reproduced.
The median improvement is modest.
But the tail of the curve is what matters.
A subset of patients — the lucky responders — get durable, sometimes permanent remissions.
They don't relapse.
The cancer doesn't come back.
It is, functionally, gone.
Why this happens, biologically, is fascinating.
When an immune system clears a tumor, it generates memory T cells.
Those memory cells persist for years, decades, lifetimes.
If even a single tumor cell tries to come back, the memory T cells recognize it and kill it before it can grow.
The immune system has been, in some sense, vaccinated against the patient's own cancer.
Once the cancer is cleared, it tends to stay cleared.
That is what ipilimumab demonstrated, for the first time, in 2010.
That immunotherapy could produce durable cures in a cancer that was previously universally fatal.
The cost, though.
Let me tell you about the cost.
Ipilimumab is also, by some distance, the most toxic checkpoint inhibitor in widespread clinical use.
Cutting the early brake on T-cell activation is a blunt instrument.
The T cells that get released don't only attack the cancer; some of them attack normal tissue.
About sixty percent of patients on ipilimumab develop some kind of immune-related adverse event — irAE in the clinical literature.
Twenty percent have severe ones.
Colitis, where T cells attack the gut lining and patients have bloody diarrhea.
Hepatitis, where T cells attack the liver.
Pneumonitis, where T cells inflame the lungs.
Skin rashes.
Endocrinopathies — the pituitary, the thyroid, the adrenals can all get hit, and the damage is often permanent.
Some patients lose their thyroid function for life.
Some have to take steroids for the rest of their lives.
A few patients die of these toxicities.
It is a real risk.
So ipilimumab, in its monotherapy form, is now mostly used in carefully selected patients — patients for whom the potential upside of a durable cure is worth the risk of severe toxicity.
In most front-line settings, it has been displaced by the gentler PD-1 inhibitors that came after.
But it is often used in combination — ipi plus nivo, as we'll discuss — and it remains a workhorse for advanced melanoma.
Mostly, though, ipilimumab is important as the proof of concept.
It showed that the principle worked.
After Yervoy, every pharmaceutical company on Earth understood that checkpoint inhibition was real, and the race for the next target was on.
